risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "As women provided consent, a member of the research team randomly allocated them to either the DA or control group, using the Bernoulli function in Excel." Comment: The article describes a random allocation procedure and specifies the use of the Bernoulli function in Excel, which is a systematic random method consistent with proper random sequence generation. This is sufficient for Low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: As women provided consent, a member of the research team randomly allocated them to either the DA or control group, using the Bernoulli function in Excel.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomly allocated to DA or control using the Bernoulli function in Excel.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "As women provided consent, a member of the research team randomly allocated them to either the DA or control group, using the Bernoulli function in Excel." Comment: The paper describes random allocation, but it does not state any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers) that would prevent the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment. Because concealment is not described, allocation concealment is unclear.

_evidence.note: The article describes random allocation by a member of the research team using the Bernoulli function in Excel, but says nothing about concealment of the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Once women in the intervention (DA) group had completed the baseline questionnaire, they were sent a link to the electronic version of the DA via email or SurveyMonkey" and "Women in both groups were followed up via phone or emailed approximately two to four weeks after the baseline questionnaire. All women in the intervention group were asked if they had read and understood the DA, and had any questions or feedback about the DA or the study topic. Women in the control group were contacted and asked if they had any questions or feedback about the questionnaire or study topic." Comment: Participants and personnel were not blinded; the intervention was directly delivered and discussed with the intervention group, while controls received no intervention. Because the main outcomes included multiple subjective self-report measures (DCS, decision certainty, ASES, HADS-A, HADS-D), knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect responses and behaviour, so the dominant risk for this domain is high.

outcome_categories: DCS subscales: informed, values clarity, support, uncertainty, effective decision
subjective_outcomes: DCS subscales: informed, values clarity, support, uncertainty, effective decision

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The questionnaire was identical to the one completed in the pre-intervention phase except for the exclusion of demographic questions." and "The DCS is a 16-item self-report questionnaire" and "The ASES provides a measure of the patient’s perceived ability..." and "The HADS consists of 14 items related to feelings of anxiety and depression" Comment: Outcome assessment was by patient self-report (the patient is the assessor). Because participants were aware of their allocation (the study describes a DA group versus control and does not report participant blinding), knowledge of allocation could influence responses on multiple subjective outcomes (knowledge, decisional conflict, self-efficacy, anxiety, depression, decision certainty). Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of these unblinded subjective outcomes makes detection bias high, even though the assessments were collected via standardized questionnaires.

Quote: "A total of 188 women consented to participate in the study. Forty-four (28 DA; 16 Control) participants did not complete pre or post questionnaire and after a number of efforts to contact them, were assumed to have withdrawn from the study. The final sample of 144 participants consisted of 78 women ... in the DA group, and 66 women ... in the control group." Comment: Post-randomization attrition on the analysed questionnaire outcomes was 44/188 (23.4%) overall, with marked imbalance between arms (DA 16/94 lost vs Control 28/94 lost; differential loss 12.8 percentage points). Analyses were complete-case with no imputation, so the observed differential and >20% loss could bias the results for the outcomes of interest.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “28 DA ... participants did not complete pre or post questionnaire and after a number of efforts to contact them, were assumed to have withdrawn from the study.”
“16 Control ... participants did not complete pre or post questionnaire and after a number of efforts to contact them, were assumed to have withdrawn from the study.”

_evidence.notes: The flow numbers are from the overall study sample and apply to the pre/post questionnaire outcomes reported (PiRAQ, DCS, ASES, HADS, etc.). No imputation method was described; analyses were conducted on the final sample of 144 participants.
